---
title: "9 · Forecast of the soil state"
---

The twin is driven forward from a rainfall forecast to produce the **antecedent hydromechanical
state** — soil moisture, water table, near-surface stiffness — that the downstream hazard models
consume. The physics is the same closed water budget that produces the hindcast
([Physics — Hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd)); only the forcing changes.

The product is the antecedent state, **not** a flood hydrograph: the budget *generates* runoff and
interflow as source terms and hands them to a surface-routing model
([Hazard integration](07-hazard-integration.qmd)). The value of the antecedent state is that a
saturated subsurface — a water table already raised by a winter atmospheric-river sequence — is what
turns the *next* storm into a landslide or a liquefaction-susceptible condition.

## Architecture: rainfall is a file, not an import

The AI weather model requires a GPU; the soil physics is pure NumPy and costs nothing. The two are
therefore separated by a **file boundary** rather than a function call:

```
GPU (Tillicum H200)   FuXi  ──►  s3://gaia/soil-twin/forecast/fuxi/<init>.zarr
                                            │
anywhere (no GPU) ──────────────────────────┴──►  water budget ──► θ, water table ──► Vs
```

`src/data/fetch_earth2studio.py` is the only module that imports earth2studio. Everything downstream
consumes a `ForecastForcing` (`src/models/forecast.py`) and is indifferent to whether the rain came
from an AI model, a reanalysis, or a scenario. Substituting the source changes one flag.

This boundary is what makes the forecast *source-agnostic*: the same engine produces a hindcast from
observed PRISM rainfall (a perfect-forcing baseline that isolates the physics from weather-forecast
error) and a forecast from an AI model. The deployment mechanics — model selection, GPU execution,
object staging — are an implementation concern and are set out in
[Appendix A](A-compute-and-deployment.qmd).

## What the forecast predicts

::: {.callout-important}
## θ and dv/v are event detectors; the water table is an integrator
Soil moisture and the shallow dv/v band respond to **individual storms** within days and decay between
them. The water table **integrates** those storms into a single cumulative rise and carries the memory,
with no trace of their separateness.

**Vs30 barely moves** even when the moisture signal is large, because the vadose root zone is only
$d/30$ of the averaging depth: a 1 m root zone changing 6% in velocity moves Vs30 by ~0.2%.

**dv/v sees the storm; Vs30 mostly does not.** The observables answer different questions and must be
scored against different data — dv/v against event *timing*, wells against the cumulative *antecedent
state*. This is the argument for the seismic network, rather than the well network, as the instrument
for short-lead forecasting.
:::

![**The twin over a wet season** (western Cascades, 90 m, daily), driven by observed PRISM daily
rainfall and corrected each frame by the BLUE assimilation of [chapter 7](04-assimilation.qmd). Soil
moisture and the shallow dv/v band track individual storms; the water table integrates them. Two
**live, independent** measurement streams drive the correction: the water-table panel is pulled toward
the in-domain NWIS wells (red circles) that report a depth-to-water that month — up to 91 in the winter
field-measurement campaigns, with each site's own seasonal climatology filling the gaps between reports
— and the soil-moisture panel toward the three in-domain SNOTEL θ stations (red triangles), assimilated
as an anomaly about each station's window mean. A synthetic deep/shallow dv/v is also assimilated at the
seismic stations, but because it is derived from the model state its update is a self-consistency check
that adds no independent information; the real signal comes from the wells and SNOTEL. Measured (rather
than synthetic) dv/v is the remaining stream to bring in.](assets/twin_wetseason.gif){#fig-twin}

## Forecast horizon and skill

The forcing model (FuXi) is a cascade trained for 5-, 10- and 15-day leads, so the twin forecasts the
soil state over **+1 … +15 days**. Skill is a function of lead time, and measuring it requires more than
one forecast: a **rolling weekly initialisation** across the wet season yields ~29 forecasts, each
verified over its own lead window.

Two scoring paths follow from the different roles of the states:

| forecast | scored against | measures |
|---|---|---|
| FuXi precipitation | PRISM daily (and HRRR analysis) | **weather** skill by lead |
| forecast θ, water table, Vs | seismic dv/v; wells; gauges | **soil-state** skill by lead |

Because θ and dv/v are event detectors while the water table is an integrator, short-lead skill is
properly scored against dv/v and event *timing*, and long-lead skill against the cumulative
*antecedent state* the wells and gauges observe. Scoring both against a single metric would confuse the
two.

## Precipitation forcing

The forcing is deliberately not tied to one product. Every field records its **native versus delivered
resolution**, so a coarse forecast pixel is never read as a native observation.

**Observed forcing** (hindcast, and the verification truth) is PRISM daily — gauge-anchored and the
best-resolved product for the PNW. It supplies precipitation and temperature, the latter being required
by the snow partition, which is first-order in this domain
([Physics — Hydrology](03a-physics-hydrology.qmd)).

**Forecast forcing** is FuXi via earth2studio. Two properties of it constrain what can be claimed:

::: {.callout-warning}
## The forecast under-resolves the terrain that governs the precipitation
FuXi's 0.25° grid (~28 × 19 km) **cannot resolve the Cascade crest**. Orographic enhancement is the
dominant precipitation signal in this domain, so forecast totals must be bias-corrected against the
PRISM climatology before they are trusted. This is a limit of the forcing, not of the budget.
:::

Precipitation is also the *only* forecast field the budget strictly needs beyond temperature — which is
why the choice of AI model turns entirely on whether it predicts precipitation at all. It is not safe
to assume it does ([Appendix A](A-compute-and-deployment.qmd)).
